past_medical_history: REM sleep behavior disorder, diagnosed 3 years ago after wife reported dream enactment with punching and yelling during sleep
Depression, diagnosed 2 years ago, managed with mirtazapine
Orthostatic hypotension, diagnosed 1 year ago, managed with midodrine
Chronic constipation, worsening over past 4 years
family_history: Mother had Parkinson's disease diagnosed at age 75, managed with levodopa, died at 82 of pneumonia
Father had dementia beginning at age 80, type unclear, died at 85
One sister, age 70, has essential tremor
No other known neurodegenerative disease in family

social_history.occupation: Retired architect; retired 3 years ago after 35-year career. Wife notes his design skills had declined in the final year, with difficulty with spatial planning.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife in a two-story home. Two adult children live nearby.
social_history.alcohol: Rare, 1-2 glasses of sake per month
social_history.exercise: Previously played golf twice weekly; stopped 1 year ago due to difficulty with coordination and 'seeing the ball.' Now takes short daily walks with his wife.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Fluctuating attention during the exam — for the first 10 minutes he is alert, conversational, and engaged, then becomes drowsy, stares blankly for several minutes, gives slow incoherent responses, before spontaneously becoming lucid again. When lucid: oriented to person, place, and situation but misses the exact date by 3 days. MMSE 23/30 — lost 2 points on recall (1/3 words, retrieved 1 with category cue), lost 2 points on serial sevens (3 errors, appeared to lose focus midway), lost 1 point on orientation (date), lost 2 points on intersecting pentagons (numbers clustered on right side of clock, lost spatial arrangement of pentagon copy). MoCA 19/30 with deficits in visuospatial/executive (cube copy failed, clock drawing 1/3 — numbers clustered on right half of clock face, trail-making 0/1), delayed recall (1/5 words, 2 with cues), attention (serial 7s: 3 errors, digit span forward only 4), and abstraction (0/2). Mild myoclonus in both hands observed intermittently.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial expression mildly hypomimic bilaterally. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone: mild bilateral cogwheel rigidity at wrists, SYMMETRIC — no clear lateralization. Mild bilateral bradykinesia on finger tapping and hand opening-closing, approximately symmetric. NO resting tremor observed during 15-minute observation period. Mild action tremor (postural) in both hands. Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration sense intact at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, and patellar bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly slow bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements mildly slow and decremented bilaterally, symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based gait. Slightly shuffling steps with reduced stride length bilaterally. Mildly reduced arm swing bilaterally, symmetric. Tandem gait with 2 missteps in 10 steps. Pull test: mild retropulsion, recovers in 2 steps. No freezing. Turns performed en bloc.
neurological_exam.additional: Orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 64; standing at 3 minutes BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 70 (systolic drop of 22 mmHg with only 6 bpm heart rate increase — inadequate compensatory tachycardia). Patient reports lightheadedness on standing. No inspiratory stridor. Mild myoclonus in both hands observed intermittently. Wife reports she has witnessed 'whole-body jerks' during sleep. Neuroleptic sensitivity history: PCP prescribed haloperidol 0.5 mg for hallucinations 3 months ago — patient became severely rigid, near-catatonic, and obtunded within 48 hours requiring emergency discontinuation and brief hospitalization.

patient.chief_complaint: Tremor and stiffness, seeing things that aren't there
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Kenji Takahashi is a 68-year-old right-handed retired architect who is referred to the neurology movement disorders clinic by his primary care physician for evaluation of 'Parkinson's disease.' His PCP noted bilateral tremor and rigidity at a routine visit 4 months ago and made a provisional diagnosis of PD. His wife, who accompanies him and provides the bulk of the history, paints a more complex picture. Approximately 2 years ago, Mr. Takahashi began experiencing vivid, detailed visual hallucinations. He sees children playing in the living room, a cat sleeping on the bed, and occasionally a woman standing in the corner of the bedroom. Initially he recognized these as unreal and would remark 'there are the children again,' but over the past 6 months he has begun interacting with the hallucinations — reaching out to pet the cat, setting a place at the dinner table for the woman, and becoming distressed when his wife tells him they are not real. Around the same time the hallucinations began, his wife noticed dramatic fluctuations in his cognitive state. Some days he is nearly his normal self — sharp, conversational, able to read the newspaper and discuss current events. Other days, and sometimes even within the same day, he becomes profoundly confused, stares blankly for hours, cannot follow simple instructions, and appears drowsy and disengaged despite adequate sleep. These fluctuations are unpredictable and have no clear trigger. REM sleep behavior disorder was diagnosed 3 years ago when his wife reported that he was acting out vivid dreams — punching, kicking, and shouting in his sleep. On two occasions he struck his wife during sleep. He was started on melatonin with partial improvement. Motor symptoms are a more recent development. Approximately 8 months ago, his wife noticed that his movements had become slower, his writing smaller, and he was walking with shorter steps. He has bilateral mild rigidity and bradykinesia. Notably, she has NEVER observed a resting tremor — only a mild tremor when he holds his hands out. He has had two falls in the past 6 months with brief loss of consciousness, both while standing from a seated position. These were attributed to 'vasovagal syncope' by his cardiologist after a normal echocardiogram and Holter monitor, but his wife notes the orthostatic hypotension was documented on both occasions. Most concerning to the family was an episode 3 months ago when his PCP prescribed haloperidol 0.5 mg for the hallucinations. Within 48 hours, Mr. Takahashi became severely rigid, nearly unable to move, obtunded, and had to be taken to the emergency department. The haloperidol was discontinued and he recovered over several days, but the family was frightened. His PCP subsequently referred him to neurology. He denies urinary incontinence. His constipation has been present for 4 years.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions — SLOW (normal ≥8 Hz)
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates with eye opening but is slow to reform

findings.description: Prominent posterior slow-wave activity with periodic fluctuations in background frequency. Periods of diffuse theta slowing (4-6 Hz) lasting 30-60 seconds alternate with near-normal background (7-8 Hz alpha). These fluctuations occur approximately every 3-5 minutes and are not associated with arousal changes or eye opening/closing.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is slow at 7 Hz with periods of diffuse theta slowing lasting 30-60 seconds alternating with near-normal background. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording in the awake and drowsy states.

signal_characteristics.T1: Hippocampal volumes are relatively preserved bilaterally, Scheltens medial temporal atrophy score 0-1
Mild generalized cortical atrophy appropriate for age, without focal predominance
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No focal white matter lesions. No periventricular hyperintensities beyond age-appropriate changes.

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal basal ganglia volumes and signal. Normal midbrain and pontine morphology.
signal_characteristics.SWI: No abnormal iron deposition. No putaminal changes.

volumetrics.cortical_volume: Mild generalized atrophy without focal predominance

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No vascular changes suggestive of vascular parkinsonism
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
mri.impression: Brain MRI shows mild generalized cortical atrophy without focal predominance and relatively preserved hippocampal volumes. No structural cause for parkinsonism identified. No vascular disease. The preserved hippocampi argue against Alzheimer disease as the primary cause of cognitive decline. The overall MRI appearance is nonspecific and does not distinguish between Parkinson disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, or other neurodegenerative processes. Functional imaging (DaTscan, FDG-PET) would be more informative. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus bradycardia at 58 bpm. Normal PR, QRS, and QTc intervals. No conduction abnormalities. No ST-T wave changes. Normal ECG aside from mild bradycardia.

abnormal_values_summary: CK 210 U/L (mildly elevated, likely from falls)

signal_characteristics.pattern: Bilateral reduction in putaminal and caudate dopamine transporter uptake, right greater than left putamen

additional_observations: Bilateral reduced dopamine transporter uptake confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit
The clinical context (cognitive/psychiatric symptoms preceding motor symptoms) is essential for interpretation
output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan demonstrating bilateral reduced dopamine transporter uptake in the putamina and caudates, right greater than left. The clinical history and temporal sequence of symptoms are required for differentiation. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Total sleep time 298 minutes (L), Sleep efficiency 68% % (H), REM sleep percentage 16% % TST (H), REM without atonia (RWA) Tonic: 62%, Phasic: 48% % of REM epochs (H), Dream-enactment behaviors 4 episodes observed events (H), Periodic limb movements index 16 events/hr (H).
abnormal_values_summary: REM without atonia: Tonic 62%, Phasic 48% (markedly elevated, confirms RBD) (H)
Reduced total sleep time and efficiency

additional_observations: Reduced cardiac MIBG uptake with early and delayed H/M ratios below normal
Increased washout rate reflects postganglionic sympathetic cardiac denervation
output.impression: ABNORMAL cardiac MIBG scintigraphy with reduced heart-to-mediastinum ratios (early 1.38, delayed 1.22) and increased washout rate (42%). This demonstrates postganglionic sympathetic cardiac denervation, which is a supportive biomarker for Lewy body pathology. Reduced cardiac MIBG uptake is seen in both Parkinson disease and dementia with Lewy bodies, reflecting the shared alpha-synuclein pathology affecting cardiac sympathetic neurons. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Clock Drawing 4/10 points (H), Cube Copy Failed  (H), Trail Making Test Part B Discontinued at 300 seconds seconds (H), Digit Span Forward Variable: 4-7 across trials digits (H), Judgment of Line Orientation 12/30 correct (H), Rey Complex Figure - Copy 3rd percentile percentile (H), Verbal Fluency (FAS) 22 total words (L).
abnormal_values_summary: Judgment of Line Orientation 12/30 — severely impaired

findings.location: Bilateral occipital cortex including primary visual cortex, posterior parietal cortex, and lateral temporal cortex

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Reduced FDG uptake in bilateral occipital cortex (including primary visual cortex V1) and posterior parietal cortex, with relative preservation of posterior cingulate cortex (cingulate island sign POSITIVE)
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Occipital-predominant hypometabolism with cingulate island sign — classic DLB metabolic pattern

output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bilateral occipital hypometabolism including primary visual cortex, with a positive cingulate island sign (relative preservation of posterior cingulate metabolism). This metabolic pattern is classic for dementia with Lewy bodies and is distinct from the temporoparietal hypometabolism with posterior cingulate involvement seen in Alzheimer disease. The cingulate island sign has high specificity for differentiating DLB from AD. Clinical correlation recommended.

differential.key_distinguishing: Cognitive symptoms (hallucinations, fluctuating cognition) preceded motor symptoms by more than 1 year — the '1-year rule' in consensus criteria favors DLB over PDD

Prominent visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and neuroleptic sensitivity all developed BEFORE significant motor parkinsonism — PD diagnosis does not account for the cognitive/psychiatric presentation
Preserved hippocampi on MRI, visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, RBD, DaTscan abnormality, and occipital hypometabolism with cingulate island sign on FDG-PET all argue strongly against AD
No vertical gaze palsy, no prominent axial rigidity, visual hallucinations and fluctuating cognition are atypical for PSP

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant focusing on temporal sequence of cognitive, psychiatric, and motor symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Visual hallucinations and fluctuating cognition preceded motor symptoms by >1 year; RBD preceded all symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including assessment of cognition (especially visuospatial), parkinsonism (symmetry, tremor type), and attention fluctuations
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Fluctuating attention, symmetric parkinsonism without rest tremor, visuospatial dysfunction, myoclonus

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes and assess hippocampal volumes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved hippocampi arguing against AD, mild generalized atrophy

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to confirm dopaminergic deficit
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced striatal uptake confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit — indicative biomarker for DLB

optimal_actions.action: STOP haloperidol immediately — document neuroleptic sensitivity as supportive feature
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Neuroleptic sensitivity is a supportive feature of DLB and is potentially life-threatening

optimal_actions.action: Consider cholinesterase inhibitor (rivastigmine preferred) as first-line for cognitive and psychiatric symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cholinesterase inhibitors are the treatment of choice for DLB — may improve cognition, hallucinations, and behavioral symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Assess for autonomic dysfunction including orthostatic hypotension
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Orthostatic hypotension documented — supportive feature of DLB

critical_actions: Recognize core DLB features: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, RBD
Order DaTscan to confirm dopaminergic deficit
Document neuroleptic sensitivity as a supportive feature
STOP haloperidol — neuroleptic sensitivity is life-threatening in DLB
Consider cholinesterase inhibitor as first-line for cognitive and psychiatric symptoms
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing PD without assessing cognitive and psychiatric symptoms
Attributing hallucinations to delirium without considering DLB
key_reasoning_points: The '1-year rule': cognitive/psychiatric symptoms preceded motor symptoms by more than 1 year, favoring DLB over PDD
Core DLB features present: fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and RBD
Neuroleptic sensitivity is a supportive feature highly suggestive of DLB
Symmetric parkinsonism without prominent rest tremor is more common in DLB than PD
EEG showing posterior slowing with fluctuating pattern is characteristic of DLB
Preserved hippocampal volumes on MRI argue against AD
Occipital hypometabolism and cingulate island sign on FDG-PET are pathognomonic for DLB

red_herrings.intended_effect: Anchors the agent to a PD diagnosis
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Motor symptoms appeared after cognitive/psychiatric symptoms, violating the temporal sequence expected for PD
Family member may have had DLB misdiagnosed as PD — a common historical confusion since DLB was not widely recognized until the 1990s
DaTscan is positive in BOTH PD and DLB — it confirms Lewy body pathology but does not distinguish between them

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Differentiating dementia with Lewy bodies from Parkinson disease in a patient referred with a PD diagnosis but whose cognitive/psychiatric symptoms preceded motor symptoms
metadata.educational_value: Teaches recognition of core and supportive features of DLB, the 1-year rule for DLB vs PDD, neuroleptic sensitivity, and the role of multimodal biomarkers in diagnosis
metadata.key_teaching_point: When visual hallucinations, fluctuating cognition, and RBD precede motor parkinsonism by more than 1 year, DLB should be diagnosed rather than PD — even if a DaTscan is positive
